questions.question:
While inserting the elements 71, 65, 84, 69, 67, 83 in an empty binary search tree (BST) in the sequence shown, the element in the lowest level is
A. 65
B. 67
C. 69
D. 83
questions.to_find: Which element ends up at the lowest (deepest) level of the BST after the given insertion sequence?

understand.plain: We insert 6 numbers one by one into an empty BST following standard BST rules. Each new element is placed by comparing it with the root and going left if smaller, right if larger, recursively. We need to find which element sits deepest in the resulting tree.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where every left child is less than its parent and every right child is greater.
keywords.example: Insert 5 then 3: 3 becomes left child of 5.

keywords.explain: The depth of a node is the number of edges from the root. The lowest level has the greatest depth.
keywords.example: Root is at depth 0; its children at depth 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='210' cy='30' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>71</text><circle cx='120' cy='100' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>65</text><circle cx='300' cy='100' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>84</text><circle cx='170' cy='170' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='176' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>69</text><circle cx='240' cy='170' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='176' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>83</text><circle cx='130' cy='240' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='246' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>67</text><line x1='210' y1='52' x2='142' y2='78' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='210' y1='52' x2='278' y2='78' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='120' y1='122' x2='148' y2='148' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='300' y1='122' x2='262' y2='148' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='170' y1='192' x2='148' y2='218' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='210' y='258' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>67 is at depth 4 (lowest level)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: BST with root 71, left child 65, right child 84. 65 has right child 69. 84 has left child 83. 69 has left child 67. So 67 is at depth 4, the deepest node.

given.aim: Build a BST by inserting 71, 65, 84, 69, 67, 83 in order and identify the deepest node.

terms.connects: Determines the structure of the BST

terms.meaning: The node(s) at the maximum depth in the tree
terms.example: In a skewed tree, the last inserted node is usually deepest
terms.connects: We trace each insertion to its final position and count depth

terms.meaning: Left subtree has values less than root; right subtree has values greater
terms.example: Insert 69 after 65: 69 > 65 so goes to right of 65
terms.connects: Guides where each new element is placed

given.plan: Simulate the BST insertions step by step. Track the depth of each inserted node. The node with the greatest depth is the answer.

steps.title: Insert all elements and build the tree
steps.formula_raw: if key < node.key go left, else go right
steps.apply:
Insert 71: root at depth 0.
Insert 65: 65 < 71 -> left child of 71, depth 1.
Insert 84: 84 > 71 -> right child of 71, depth 1.
Insert 69: 69 < 71 -> go left to 65; 69 > 65 -> right child of 65, depth 2.
Insert 67: 67 < 71 -> 65; 67 > 65 -> 69; 67 < 69 -> left child of 69, depth 3.
Insert 83: 83 > 71 -> 84; 83 < 84 -> left child of 84, depth 2.
steps.note: Each step follows the BST insertion rule — compare and descend left or right.

steps.title: Find the deepest node
steps.apply: Node depths: 71->0, 65->1, 84->1, 69->2, 83->2, 67->3. Maximum depth = 3, achieved by 67.
steps.note: 67 is the only node at depth 3, making it the element at the lowest level.

solution.result: The element at the lowest level is 67. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Standard BST insertion: compare key with current node, recurse left if smaller, right if larger. Node depth increases by 1 at each level.

questions.question:
The number of ways in which the numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 can be inserted in an empty binary search tree, such that the resulting tree has height 6, is _________.
Note: The height of a tree with a single node is 0.
questions.to_find: Count insertion sequences of {1,2,3,4,5,6,7} that produce a BST of height exactly 6 (a completely skewed/degenerate tree).

understand.plain: We have 7 distinct numbers. We want to insert them into an empty BST in some order so that the resulting tree is completely skewed (every node has at most one child), giving maximum height 6. We need to count how many such insertion orders exist.

keywords.explain: The number of edges on the longest path from root to a leaf. A single node has height 0.
keywords.example: A chain of 7 nodes has height 6.

keywords.explain: A BST where every node has only one child — resembles a linked list. Occurs when elements are inserted in sorted or reverse-sorted order, or any order where each new element is always the current min or max.
keywords.example: Inserting 1,2,3,4,5,6,7 in order creates a right-skewed tree of height 6.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='60' cy='20' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='26' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>root</text><line x1='60' y1='38' x2='90' y2='52' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='100' cy='65' r='18' class='bg'/><line x1='100' y1='83' x2='130' y2='97' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='140' cy='110' r='18' class='bg'/><line x1='140' y1='128' x2='170' y2='142' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='180' cy='155' r='18' class='bg'/><line x1='180' y1='173' x2='210' y2='187' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='220' cy='200' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='115' class='sub mut' dx='30'>...</text><text x='30' y='120' class='sub ink'>Each node has</text><text x='30' y='138' class='sub ink'>only one child</text><text x='30' y='156' class='sub ink'>=> height = 6</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A skewed BST where each node has exactly one child, forming a chain of 7 nodes with height 6.

given.aim: Count insertion orderings of {1,2,3,4,5,6,7} that yield a BST of height 6.

terms.meaning: Every node has exactly one child (or none for the last), forming a path/chain.
terms.example: 1->2->3->4->5->6->7 is a right-skewed chain of height 6.
terms.connects: Forces us to count only sequences producing completely skewed trees.

terms.term: When does a new node deepen the chain?
terms.meaning: A new element always extends the chain if it is either the current minimum or current maximum of uninserted elements.
terms.example: If 4 is root, inserting 3 (current min) extends left, inserting 5 (current max) extends right.
terms.connects: At each of the 6 subsequent insertions, there are exactly 2 valid choices.

terms.meaning: At each step (after the root) the new element must be either the current global min or max to keep the tree skewed.
terms.example: After root, 6 remaining insertions each have 2 choices -> 2^6 = 64.
terms.connects: Gives the total count of valid sequences.

given.plan: For a BST with n nodes to have height n-1 (completely skewed), at every insertion step the new key must be either the smallest or largest among all keys inserted so far (extending one end of the current range). The first element (root) can be any of the 7 values but is determined by the sequence; the remaining 6 positions each independently have 2 choices (new min or new max). Hence count = 2^(7-1) = 2^6 = 64.

steps.title: Understand condition for height n-1
steps.formula_raw: height = n - 1 iff every node has exactly one child (degenerate tree)
steps.apply: For 7 nodes, height 6 means the tree is a path: each inserted element must either be less than all previously inserted elements (goes to the leftmost leaf) or greater than all previously inserted elements (goes to the rightmost leaf). Any other insertion would create a branching node and reduce the final height below 6.
steps.note: This is equivalent to building the sequence by repeatedly picking either the current minimum or maximum of the remaining elements.

steps.title: Count valid sequences
steps.apply: Think of it as: the complete sequence is determined by choosing, at each of 7 positions, whether the next element added to the path is the global min or global max of unplaced elements. The first placement is forced (only 1 element chosen), but positions 2 through 7 each have 2 options. Actually more precisely: consider all 7 elements. The sequence must be such that each element is at an extreme (min or max) of the remaining uninserted set at the time of insertion. For 7 elements this gives 2^(7-1) = 2^6 = 64 sequences.
steps.note: Verification: for n=2 elements {1,2}, valid sequences are (1,2) and (2,1), count = 2^1 = 2. For n=3 elements {1,2,3}: valid are 1,2,3; 1,3,2; 3,2,1; 3,1,2 — count = 4 = 2^2. Pattern holds.

solution.result: The number of valid insertion sequences is 2^6 = 64.

questions.formula_note: For n keys, the number of insertion orders yielding a completely degenerate BST (height n-1) is 2^(n-1). Each step after the first must add either the current minimum or maximum.

questions.question:
Let T be a binary search tree with 15 nodes. The minimum and maximum possible heights of T are:
Note: The height of a tree with a single node is 0.
A. 4 and 14 respectively.
B. 3 and 14 respectively.
C. 4 and 15 respectively.
D. 3 and 15 respectively.
questions.to_find: Minimum and maximum heights of a BST with 15 nodes (height of single node = 0).

understand.plain: A BST with 15 nodes can be arranged in many shapes. The minimum height occurs when the tree is as balanced as possible (complete binary tree), while the maximum height occurs when the tree is completely skewed (like a linked list). We need to calculate both extremes.

keywords.explain: Achieved when the BST is a complete/perfect binary tree — all levels fully filled.
keywords.example: 15 nodes fill a perfect binary tree of 4 levels (depths 0-3), so height = 3.

keywords.explain: Achieved when the BST is completely skewed — each node has exactly one child except the leaf.
keywords.example: Inserting 1,2,...,15 in order gives a right-skewed BST of height 14.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='100' y='18' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Min Height = 3</text><circle cx='100' cy='40' r='14' class='ok'/><circle cx='70' cy='75' r='14' class='ok'/><circle cx='130' cy='75' r='14' class='ok'/><circle cx='55' cy='110' r='14' class='ok'/><circle cx='85' cy='110' r='14' class='ok'/><circle cx='115' cy='110' r='14' class='ok'/><circle cx='145' cy='110' r='14' class='ok'/><circle cx='47' cy='145' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='63' cy='145' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='77' cy='145' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='93' cy='145' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='107' cy='145' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='123' cy='145' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='137' cy='145' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='153' cy='145' r='12' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>15 nodes, height 3</text><text x='320' y='18' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Max Height = 14</text><circle cx='200' cy='40' r='14' class='no'/><circle cx='220' cy='75' r='14' class='no'/><circle cx='240' cy='110' r='14' class='no'/><text x='265' y='130' class='sub mut'>... 15 nodes</text><text x='320' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>skewed, height 14</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Left side shows a perfect binary tree with 15 nodes arranged in 4 levels (height 3). Right side shows a skewed tree with 15 nodes in a chain (height 14).

given.aim: Find minimum and maximum heights of a BST with 15 nodes, where height of a single node is 0.

terms.example: 15 = 2^4 - 1, which is a perfect binary tree number.
terms.connects: 15 = 2^4 - 1 means a perfect binary tree with 4 levels (height 3) is achievable.

terms.meaning: floor(log_2(n)) for a complete binary tree.
n - 1 for a completely skewed tree.

given.plan: Use the formulas: min height = floor(log_2(n)) and max height = n - 1. With n = 15: min = floor(log_2(15)) = 3, max = 14.

steps.title: Compute minimum height
steps.apply: n = 15. log_2(15) = log_2(16) - something = 4 - epsilon ≈ 3.907. floor(3.907) = 3. Also verifiable: 2^3 = 8 <= 15 <= 2^4 - 1 = 15, so a perfect binary tree of height 3 has exactly 15 nodes. Thus h_min = 3.
steps.note: 15 = 2^4 - 1 is a perfect number, so the minimum-height BST is in fact a perfect binary tree.

steps.title: Compute maximum height
steps.apply: A completely skewed (degenerate) BST has each node with exactly one child. The root is at depth 0 and the leaf at depth n-1 = 14. So h_max = 15 - 1 = 14.
steps.note: Maximum height is n-1 = 14 (not n), because height is measured in edges and the root itself is at height 0.

solution.result: Minimum height = 3, Maximum height = 14. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: For a BST with n nodes: minimum height = floor(log_2(n)) (balanced/complete tree), maximum height = n-1 (degenerate/skewed tree). Height is counted in edges (single node = 0).

questions.to_find: Post-order traversal of the BST given its pre-order traversal 12, 8, 6, 2, 7, 9, 10, 15, 19, 17, 20.

understand.plain: Given the pre-order traversal of a BST, we can uniquely reconstruct the tree (because BST property determines left/right children). Once the tree is reconstructed, we perform post-order traversal (left subtree, right subtree, root) to get the answer.

keywords.explain: Visit root first, then left subtree, then right subtree.
keywords.example: Pre-order of a tree rooted at 5 with children 3(L) and 7(R): 5, 3, 7.

keywords.explain: Visit left subtree first, then right subtree, then root.
In pre-order, the first element is the root. All elements less than root in the pre-order sequence form the left subtree; all greater form the right subtree. This splits the remaining sequence.
keywords.example: Pre-order: 12, 8, 6, ... -> root=12. Elements < 12 are 8,6,2,7,9,10; elements > 12 are 15,19,17,20.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 310' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='240' cy='28' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='34' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>12</text><circle cx='130' cy='90' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>8</text><circle cx='350' cy='90' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>15</text><circle cx='70' cy='155' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>6</text><circle cx='190' cy='155' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>9</text><circle cx='420' cy='155' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='420' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>19</text><circle cx='40' cy='220' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='226' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><circle cx='100' cy='220' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='226' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>7</text><circle cx='230' cy='220' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='226' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>10</text><circle cx='390' cy='220' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='390' y='226' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>17</text><circle cx='450' cy='220' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='450' y='226' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>20</text><line x1='240' y1='48' x2='150' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='240' y1='48' x2='330' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='110' x2='90' y2='135' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='110' x2='170' y2='135' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='350' y1='110' x2='400' y2='135' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='70' y1='175' x2='50' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='70' y1='175' x2='90' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='190' y1='175' x2='220' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='420' y1='175' x2='400' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='420' y1='175' x2='440' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: BST structure: root 12, left subtree rooted at 8 (children 6 and 9), right subtree rooted at 15 (right child 19). Node 6 has children 2 and 7; node 9 has right child 10; node 19 has children 17 and 20.

given.aim: Reconstruct BST from pre-order traversal and find post-order traversal.

terms.meaning: 12, 8, 6, 2, 7, 9, 10, 15, 19, 17, 20 — the order of visiting root, left, right.
Elements less than root go to left subtree; greater go to right subtree.
terms.example: Root=12: left subtree has {2,6,7,8,9,10}; right has {15,17,19,20}.

terms.meaning: Visit all of left subtree, then all of right subtree, then the root node.
terms.example: Post-order of tree with root R, left L, right Rt = post(L) + post(Rt) + R.
terms.connects: Applied recursively after reconstruction gives the answer.

given.plan: Insert each element of the pre-order sequence into a BST (since pre-order of BST uniquely defines the tree). Then perform post-order traversal: for each node, output left subtree post-order, then right subtree post-order, then the node itself.

steps.title: Reconstruct the BST from pre-order
steps.formula_raw: Pre-order: root, left subtree, right subtree
steps.apply:
Root = 12.
Left subtree of 12 (elements < 12 in pre-order before elements > 12): 8, 6, 2, 7, 9, 10.
Right subtree of 12: 15, 19, 17, 20.

Left subtree rooted at 8:
  Root=8. Elements < 8: 6,2,7. Elements > 8 (and < 12): 9,10.
  Left of 8 -> root=6: elements < 6 are {2} (left), elements in (6,8) are {7} (right). So 6 has left child 2, right child 7.
  Right of 8 -> root=9: elements > 9 (and < 12) = {10}. So 9 has right child 10.

Right subtree rooted at 15:
  Root=15. Elements > 15: 19,17,20.
  Right of 15 -> root=19: elements < 19 (and > 15) = {17} (left); elements > 19 = {20} (right). So 19 has left child 17, right child 20.
steps.note: The BST: 12 -> left:8, right:15; 8 -> left:6, right:9; 6 -> left:2, right:7; 9 -> right:10; 15 -> right:19; 19 -> left:17, right:20.

steps.title: Compute post-order traversal
steps.apply:
Post-order of left subtree of 12:
  Post(6): post(2)=2, post(7)=7, root=6 -> 2,7,6
  Post(9): post(10)=10, root=9 -> 10,9
  Post(8): post(6)=2,7,6; post(9)=10,9; root=8 -> 2,7,6,10,9,8

Post-order of right subtree of 12:
  Post(19): post(17)=17, post(20)=20, root=19 -> 17,20,19
  Post(15): no left child, post(19)=17,20,19; root=15 -> 17,20,19,15

Full post-order: 2,7,6,10,9,8,17,20,19,15,12
steps.note: The key distinction between options B and D is whether 15 or 19 comes first in the right subtree post-order; since 19 is the child of 15, post-order gives ...19,15,12 not ...15,19,12.

questions.formula_note: BST pre-order uniquely determines the tree structure. Post-order is computed by recursively applying: left subtree post-order + right subtree post-order + root.

questions.question:
In a balanced binary search tree with n elements, what is the worst case time complexity of reporting all elements in range [k1, k2]? Assume that the number of reported elements is k.
A. Theta(log(n))
B. Theta(log(n) + k)
C. Theta(k * log(n))
D. Theta(n)
questions.to_find: Worst case time complexity for a range query [k1, k2] in a balanced BST with n elements, reporting k elements.

understand.plain: We want to find all elements in a balanced BST that fall between k1 and k2. This involves first searching for where k1 is in the tree (taking O(log n) time), and then traversing/reporting all k elements in the range. The total time is the sum of these two phases.

keywords.explain: Retrieving all elements in a data structure whose values fall within a given range [k1, k2].
keywords.example: Find all employees with salary between 50000 and 80000 in a BST of salaries.

keywords.explain: A BST where the height is O(log n), ensuring search operations take O(log n) time.
keywords.example: AVL tree or Red-Black tree with n nodes has height Theta(log n).

keywords.explain: The complexity depends on the size k of the output in addition to the input size n.
keywords.example: Theta(log n + k) means: log n to find the start, k to list all results.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='160' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Phase 1: Search for k1</text><text x='90' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Time: O(log n)</text><rect x='210' y='10' width='160' height='50' rx='8' class='ok'/><text x='290' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>Phase 2: Report k</text><text x='290' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>elements</text><text x='290' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'></text><rect x='80' y='80' width='220' height='50' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='190' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Total: Theta(log n + k)</text><text x='190' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Search + Output</text><line x1='90' y1='60' x2='130' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='290' y1='60' x2='250' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-phase diagram: Phase 1 searches for k1 in O(log n); Phase 2 reports k elements. Total = Theta(log n + k).

given.aim: Determine the worst-case time complexity of a range query [k1, k2] on a balanced BST with n elements, where k elements are in the range.

terms.meaning: Total number of elements in the balanced BST.
terms.connects: Height of balanced BST = O(log n), so search takes O(log n).

terms.meaning: Number of elements in the output (those satisfying k1 <= element <= k2).
terms.example: If 50 elements are in range, k = 50.
terms.connects: Reporting each element takes O(1) per element, so O(k) total for output.

terms.meaning: Search for k1 in the BST (O(log n)), then do an in-order traversal reporting all nodes with values between k1 and k2, stopping when value exceeds k2.
terms.example: Start in-order traversal from the node >= k1; output until node > k2.
terms.connects: Navigation = O(log n), output = O(k). Total = O(log n + k).

given.plan: The range reporting algorithm on a balanced BST has two costs: (1) finding the starting point k1 (or nearest node >= k1) requires traversing from root to a leaf, costing O(log n); (2) reporting each of the k elements in [k1, k2] via in-order traversal costs O(k). The total is Theta(log n + k).

steps.title: Cost to locate the range start
steps.formula_raw: Search time in balanced BST = O(log n)
steps.apply: Starting from the root, search for k1 by going left if the current node > k1 and right if current node < k1. This takes at most O(height) = O(log n) steps in a balanced BST. This is the navigation/search phase.
steps.note: Even if k1 is not present, we reach a leaf in O(log n) steps and the algorithm handles the boundary correctly.

steps.title: Cost to report all k elements
steps.apply: Once we have found the first element >= k1, we perform an in-order traversal from that point, outputting each node until we exceed k2. There are exactly k such elements, each visited once, so this costs O(k).
steps.note: We also need O(log n) to backtrack/navigate through the tree during in-order traversal, but this is dominated or part of the same O(log n) term.

steps.title: Combine total cost
steps.formula_raw: Total = O(log n) + O(k) = O(log n + k)
steps.apply: Both phases together give Theta(log n + k). This is tight: we cannot avoid O(log n) for the search and cannot avoid O(k) for reporting k elements. Hence the complexity is Theta(log n + k).
steps.note: Option C, O(k * log n), would be the cost if we searched separately for each of the k elements — that would be wasteful. The in-order traversal approach gives the tight bound Theta(log n + k).

solution.result: Worst case time complexity = Theta(log(n) + k). Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Range reporting in a balanced BST: O(log n) to find the start of the range + O(k) to enumerate the k results = Theta(log n + k). This is an output-sensitive algorithm.

questions.question:
The preorder traversal of a binary search tree is 15, 10, 12, 11, 20, 18, 16, 19. Which one of the following is the postorder traversal of the tree?
A. 10, 12, 15, 16, 18, 19, 20, 11
B. 11, 12, 10, 16, 19, 18, 20, 15
C. 10, 11, 12, 20, 19, 18, 16, 15
D. 19, 16, 18, 20, 10, 11, 12, 15
questions.to_find: Post-order traversal of the BST given its pre-order traversal 15, 10, 12, 11, 20, 18, 16, 19.

understand.plain: Given a BST's pre-order traversal, we can reconstruct the tree by inserting elements in pre-order order (since pre-order of BST uniquely determines the tree). After building the tree, we compute the post-order traversal.

keywords.explain: Visits root before subtrees. The sequence root, then all nodes in left subtree (all < root), then all nodes in right subtree (all > root).
keywords.example: Pre-order 15, 10, 12... -> 15 is root, 10 < 15 so it's in left subtree, 12 > 10 but < 15 so still left subtree.

keywords.explain: Left subtree first, right subtree second, then root.
keywords.example: Tree: 15(root), left=10, right=20 -> post-order: post(left), post(right), 15.

understand.visual_alt: BST structure: root 15, left child 10, right child 20. Node 10 has right child 12. Node 12 has left child 11. Node 20 has left child 18. Node 18 has children 16 (left) and 19 (right).

given.aim: Reconstruct BST from pre-order 15, 10, 12, 11, 20, 18, 16, 19 and find post-order traversal.

terms.example: Elements < 15: {10, 12, 11} form left subtree; elements > 15: {20, 18, 16, 19} form right subtree.

terms.meaning: 10, 12, 11 — elements less than 15 in the pre-order sequence.
terms.example: Root of left subtree = 10. Elements > 10 (and < 15) = {12, 11} form right subtree of 10. Elements < 10 = none (left subtree of 10 is empty).
terms.connects: Builds the left branch: 10 -> right: 12 -> left: 11.

terms.example: Root = 20. Elements < 20 (and > 15) = {18, 16, 19} form left subtree of 20. 18 is root of that; 16 < 18 (left), 19 > 18 (right).
terms.connects: Builds the right branch: 20 -> left: 18 -> children: 16 (left), 19 (right).

given.plan: Use the pre-order sequence to reconstruct the BST. The first element is root; split remaining elements by comparing to root to find left and right subtrees. Apply recursively. Then compute post-order (left, right, root) on the reconstructed tree.

steps.title: Reconstruct the BST
steps.formula_raw: Pre-order: root | left subtree elements | right subtree elements
steps.apply:
Root = 15.
Left subtree (elements < 15 in pre-order): 10, 12, 11.
  Root of left = 10. Elements > 10 (and < 15): 12, 11.
    Root = 12. Elements < 12 (and > 10): 11. No elements > 12 (and < 15).
    So 12 has left child 11, no right child.
  10 has no left child (no elements < 10), right child = 12.

Right subtree (elements > 15 in pre-order): 20, 18, 16, 19.
  Root of right = 20. Elements < 20 (and > 15): 18, 16, 19.
    Root = 18. Elements < 18 (and > 15): 16. Elements > 18 (and < 20): 19.
    So 18 has left child 16, right child 19.
  20 has left child = 18, no right child.

Final tree: 15->(left:10->(right:12->(left:11))), (right:20->(left:18->(left:16, right:19)))
steps.note: Always verify: in-order of this BST should be sorted: 10,11,12,15,16,18,19,20. Check: in-order = 11,12,10,15,16,18,19,20? No. Correct in-order: left-subtree in-order (10's left is null, then 10, then 12's left=11, then 12) = 10,11,12, then root 15, then right subtree in-order (18's left=16, 18, 18's right=19, 20) = 16,18,19,20. Full in-order: 10,11,12,15,16,18,19,20. Sorted. Tree is correct.

steps.title: Compute post-order traversal
steps.apply:
Left subtree of 15 (rooted at 10):
  Post(12): left=11 (leaf, post=11), no right, root=12 -> 11,12
  Post(10): no left, right subtree post=11,12; root=10 -> 11,12,10

Right subtree of 15 (rooted at 20):
  Post(18): left=16 (leaf, post=16), right=19 (leaf, post=19); root=18 -> 16,19,18
  Post(20): left subtree post=16,19,18; no right; root=20 -> 16,19,18,20

Full post-order: 11,12,10,16,19,18,20,15
steps.note: Compare with options: B is exactly 11,12,10,16,19,18,20,15. This matches our computation.

questions.formula_note: BST pre-order uniquely reconstructs the tree (BST property determines left/right partitioning). Post-order = left subtree post-order + right subtree post-order + root, applied recursively.
